items.title: Define the label before collecting data
items.avoid: Gathering features first and deciding what y means after the fact.
items.prefer: Write a precise label definition (what counts as fraud, churn, positive class) and annotation guidelines before labeling.
items.why: Consistent y across annotators is the foundation of a usable supervision signal.
items.example: Churn label = no login for 90 days AND subscription cancelled, not just 'inactive 7 days'.

items.title: Split data before any preprocessing that uses labels
items.avoid: Computing global statistics or feature selection using the full dataset including future test labels.
items.prefer: Train/val/test split first; fit scalers, encoders, and selectors on training data only.
items.why: Prevents label information from leaking into features via whole-dataset statistics.
items.example: Fit StandardScaler on X_train only, then transform X_val and X_test with training mean/std.

items.title: Match inference inputs to training features
items.avoid: Deploying a model trained on engineered features that are unavailable or computed differently at serving time.
items.prefer: Document the exact feature pipeline used during training and replicate it in production for new x.
items.why: Supervised models map a specific feature space; distribution or schema mismatch breaks predictions.
items.example: Training used log(price) but API sends raw price — predictions will be wrong until schemas align.

items.title: Budget for label quality, not just quantity
items.avoid: Maximizing row count with inconsistent crowd labels and no adjudication.
items.prefer: Inter-annotator agreement checks, expert review on edge cases, and periodic relabel audits.
items.why: Noisy y teaches the wrong function; a smaller clean set often beats a large noisy one.
items.example: Sample 200 disagreed examples for expert relabel before adding 10k more crowd labels.
